_comment: AEGIS Redis service descriptor. Used for: (1) cache of agent + policy + trust-score reads, (2) BullMQ queues (BATE, webhook delivery, audit-pending DLQ), (3) atomic spend counters (INCRBY).
_planTier: Railway Redis Hobby tier (256 MB) is enough until cache + queue depth crosses ~150 MB. Upsize before that — eviction under cache pressure forces extra Postgres reads on the verify hot path and blows the latency SLO.

deploy._replicaNote: Single instance pre-launch. Redis Sentinel / managed Upstash with auto-failover is the next step before paid GA — BullMQ does NOT degrade gracefully if Redis flaps.

volumes.name: aegis-redis-data
volumes._sizeNote: 1 GB. AOF + RDB combined under maxmemory; the volume is for durability, not capacity.

config._reference: infra/redis/redis.conf — mount this as the redis-server config. Hardening choices documented inline in that file.

envVars._provision: Railway generates on plugin creation, or roll your own with `openssl rand -base64 32`. Must be referenced by `requirepass` in redis.conf — Railway's plugin templates this automatically; if you self-host, edit redis.conf to substitute the value at deploy time.

envVars._note: Leave headroom under the plan's hard limit. allkeys-lru eviction (set in redis.conf) keeps cache hot; queue keys use namespaced TTLs so they're not victims of generic LRU pressure.

_security.tls: Recommended: enable TLS via `tls-port` once we move off the Railway plugin's internal-only network. Internal traffic on Railway is not routed over the public internet, so plain TCP is acceptable pre-launch.
_security.configRemote: redis.conf disables CONFIG via `rename-command CONFIG ""` — see redis.conf rationale. This means even an attacker with the Redis password cannot mutate config or execute SCRIPT-based RCE primitives at runtime.

_backups.aof: AOF is enabled (everysec). Restore in-place is automatic on container restart.
_backups.snapshot: RDB at 15-minute intervals as a belt-and-braces. For point-in-time restore beyond the last AOF rewrite, dump RDB to object storage on a cron — tracked under M-019 (operator backup runbook).
